AI Product · No-Code Insurance Platform

InsureFlow

A no-code AI agent builder for insurance teams.

Designed an agent-based AI Suite that lets underwriters, claims ops, and customer-support teams build, test, and deploy AI agents for insurance workflows, without writing code. Reasoning is shown in plain English; every decision is logged and replayable for audit.

Role
Lead AI Product Designer
Timeline
9 months · 2025
Domain
Underwriting · Claims · CS
Team
AI Eng · Insurance Platform · UX · Ops
Tools
Figma · Miro · FigJam
10×
Faster deployment
Time from idea → production. Pre-platform: ~6 weeks (dev sprint). Post: median 4 hours across 12 pilot users.
41%→89%
Underwriting trust
Internal 5-point trust survey of underwriters + claims ops, run pre-pilot (n=38) and post-launch (n=44). Share at top 2 trust scores.
87%
Task completion rate
% of agent runs reaching the user's defined success state (no error, no manual rescue). Measured across ~3,400 production runs.
1K+
Non-technical builders
Monthly active builders (created ≥1 agent) across 4 pilot carriers in the 9-month rollout. Pre-platform: ~0.
01 · Goal & Objective

Put insurance AI in the hands of the people who run insurance.

The goal was to remove the engineering bottleneck from AI agent creation in regulated insurance workflows, and replace it with a visual, sandbox-first platform that underwriters, claims handlers, and policy ops could use directly. Without ever touching code, and without ever exposing live applicant data to an unproven agent.

Goal 01 · Speed

Cut idea-to-production time for a new insurance agent from months to hours. Aim: 10× faster deployment than the dev-led baseline.

Goal 02 · Access

Unlock a new builder segment, non-technical underwriting / claims ops staff. Aim: 1,000+ active non-engineer builders within first year.

Goal 03 · Trust

Make AI underwriting decisions explainable and auditable. Every step traceable to source data, every rule overridable, every output replayable for compliance review.

Design Objectives
✓Visual flowchart-style canvas that mirrors how insurance ops already think (if-this-then-that).
✓Sandbox mode mandatory before any agent touches live applicant or claims data.
✓Human-in-the-loop on irreversible actions (approve / send quote / escalate).
✓Plain-English reasoning visible at every step, no black-box decisions.
✓One-click connectors to core insurance systems (Guidewire, Duck Creek, Majesco).
✓Full observability: every run logged, replayable, audit-ready for compliance.
02 · The Problem

Insurance has the ideas. Engineering had the keys.

Insurance carriers want AI agents to automate underwriting checks, triage claims, populate policy checklists, and guide customers through digital claims. Every workflow change meant a dev ticket, weeks of waiting, and a fragile agent that broke whenever a checklist updated. The gap between who understood underwriting and who could build the agents was killing innovation velocity.

Dev dependency

"Every rule change is a dev ticket. My backlog is 6 weeks long.", Underwriting Ops Lead, Global Insurer

Black box fear

"I don't know what the AI is doing between the policy PDF and the Approve/Reject decision. That makes me nervous."

No test environment

"We tested an underwriter agent on real applicant data and spilled sensitive info into the wrong queue. We need a sandbox before we'll touch applications again."

03 · Research & Empathy

22 interviews. 3 insurance mental models. 1 clear paradigm shift.

👩‍💼
Maya O’Connor
Head of Underwriting Ops · 0 coding experience
User type Power user

"I can describe underwriting logic in plain English: if risk score > X and geography is flood-zone, require manual review. I just need to translate that into a workflow without writing Python."

Visual-first Plain-English goals Afraid of errors
👨‍💻
Raj Kapoor
AI Platform Engineer · builds underwriter services
User type Internal

"I spend 60% of my time tweaking prompts and connectors for ops stakeholders. If they could do it themselves, I could focus on core-model upgrades."

Wants delegation Needs guardrails
I-01Mental model: flowcharts, not code

Insurance ops already think in if-this-then-that logic: "if claim is flood-type and loss > €50k, escalate to specialist". The design challenge is exposing agent-based complexity through familiar visual metaphors, nodes, connectors, conditions.

I-02Trust requires visibility

Underwriters trusted agents more when they could see how the risk was scored, which rules triggered, and which fields were used. Black-box underwriting had 3× higher override rates and 5× higher abandonment in pilot workflows.

I-03Model choice is a UX decision

Underwriters didn’t care about MMLU scores. They cared about "which model is better for policy-clause extraction?" or "which is safer for sensitive health data?". The UX had to abstract model choice into insurance language.

I-04Sandbox before live, always

100% of interviewees insisted: "I will not run an AI underwriter on live applications without testing on dummy data first." In insurance, sandbox mode is regulatory-grade table stakes.

04 · Task Flow & Information Architecture

Mapping the underwriter’s journey. Structuring the platform.

Before the canvas could be designed, two artefacts had to exist: a task flow that captured what an underwriter actually does across the full pipeline, and an information architecture that organised the platform around insurance mental models, not generic SaaS conventions.

Task Flow, “Build a new claims-triage agent and ship it”
User actions
System response
Decision point
Total task time: ~30 min from idea to live agent (vs 2 weeks pre-platform)
Information Architecture, InsureFlow Platform
Top-level navigation
Insurance agent types
Editor / sandbox
Building blocks
Compliance layer
05 · Visual Agent Canvas

Drag-and-drop. Connect. Deploy.

The canvas is the core of InsureFlow. Users assemble insurance agents from four block types, Brain (policy summariser, risk evaluator, fraud detector), Tools (Guidewire, Duck Creek, policy registry, CRM), Memory (rules, checklists, exclusion lists), and Actions (Approve/Reject, Route to specialist, Send quote), connected by visual edges that represent decision logic.

Interactive walkthrough below · This is a portfolio re-creation built in HTML/CSS to illustrate the flow. The production product is behind carrier NDA; private walkthrough available on request.

06 · Key Design Decisions

Designing for trust, transparency, and control.

🧠

Model selector by use case

Users pick by insurance goal, "best for policy-summarisation", "safest for sensitive health data", "most cost-efficient at high claims volume". Side-by-side comparison shows cost, latency, and hallucination risk on synthetic insurance data.

Fork: Engineering wanted raw model names (GPT-4o, Claude 3.5, Llama-3). I chose use-case framing — non-technical users couldn't translate model names to insurance outcomes.
⏸

Pause & override

Before any irreversible insurance action, Approve policy change, Send quote, Escalate high-risk claim, the agent pauses and shows a preview. Approve, edit, or cancel. Reduced override rate by 60%.

Fork: Compliance pushed for full autonomy with post-hoc audit (faster, cheaper). Underwriting pushed for checkpoints. We chose checkpoints — adoption depended on it, even at a small speed cost.
🧪

Sandbox mode

Every agent runs against synthetic policy applications, claims, and updates before going live. Sandbox shows full trace logs, confidence score, false-positive-reject rate, and over-approve risk. The single most-demanded feature from underwriting and compliance.

Fork: Original v0 shipped without sandbox — users would publish straight to staging. After two compliance escalations in week 3, sandbox became a v1 ship-blocker. Cost two weeks. Worth it.
💭

Thinking visibility

The agent shows its reasoning in plain English: "Reading policy draft section 5.3… checking for flood-zone exclusions… no exclusion found… recommending Approve with standard premium". The single feature that moved underwriting from observers to active operators of the agent.

🔌

One-click tool connectors

Guidewire, Duck Creek, Majesco-style core APIs, CRM, policy registries, quote engines, OAuth-connected in one click. Each Tool block handles insurance-specific scopes (read-only on policy data, write-only on claims status) and error states (Policy not found, Missing critical data).

📊

Observability logs

Every run logs steps, tools called, rules triggered, underwriting risk score, tokens, and latency. Users replay failed runs, adjust risk thresholds in Memory, and re-run without restarting the case. Most-used feature post-launch, more than the canvas itself.

Who pushed back, and why

Not every design choice was universally loved, and the disagreements shaped the product more than the consensus did.

  • Compliance pushed back on the visual canvas — worried it abstracted too much from the underwriting rulebook and would make rule changes hard to audit. I led a working session with Compliance and Legal to surface the exact audit requirements, then designed per-block change history and rule-source links as a direct response. Compliance signed off within one sprint. This is the kind of regulated-domain stakeholder alignment that unlocks shipping — not just design craft.
  • AI Engineering pushed back on Pause & Override — the latency cost meant agents felt slower than the previous prompt-only flow. We compromised on a "fast path" for low-stakes runs where pause was optional.
  • One carrier's CTO rejected the pilot outright on grounds of model-portability risk (didn't want to be locked to one model vendor). We added the model-selector layer largely to win this back. He’s now the loudest internal champion at that carrier.
07 · Outcomes & Impact

From dev sprints to ops-led agents in one product cycle.

Deployment time (months → hours)~10× faster
Baseline: idea-to-production cycle in the previous dev-led process took multiple sprint cycles. Post-platform, ops users could ship a working agent in a single working day.
Non-technical builders (0 → 1,000+)New user segment
Active users across pilot carriers over the 9-month rollout. Pre-platform, agent-building required engineering sprint capacity, so the segment effectively didn't exist.
Agent trust score (claims + underwriting)41% → 89%
Internal trust survey of underwriters and claims ops across pilot carriers, run pre-pilot and again post-launch. The same trust question was asked both rounds; score reflects the share of respondents at the high-trust end of the scale.
Dev time on prompt & rule changes~−70% freed
Directional estimate from the AI engineering team. Reflects time previously spent on prompt edits, rule updates, and ad-hoc agent tweaks that ops users could now do themselves on the platform.

"I built and deployed our email triage agent in an afternoon. Six months ago that would have been a two-week dev sprint.", Maya O’Connor, Head of Revenue Ops, post-launch survey

08 · Reflection

What I got wrong, and what it taught me.

Four honest takeaways from the 9-month build, each shaped by something a user, stakeholder, or my own assumption got wrong.

01

I designed for the wrong power user.

I assumed underwriters would use the tool least, since their work is rules-heavy and risk-sensitive. The opposite happened: by week 4 of the pilot, underwriters had built more agents than claims ops and customer support combined. Canvas defaults skewed toward simpler claims-triage; we re-skinned them for rules-heavy underwriting flows in v1.1. Lesson: in regulated domains, the most risk-aware users are often the most motivated builders, because they already think in workflows.

02

No-code is a trust problem, not a UI problem.

I thought a clean visual builder would be enough. It wasn't. In regulated insurance, the bar for adoption is auditability and explainability, not simplicity. The pause-and-override, the trace logs, the model-selector framing — those moved the trust score. The canvas alone never could have. Visual ≠ simple in this domain. Density is fine if it serves comprehension.

03

I shipped breadth before depth, and I'd reverse it.

v1 had a wide library of generic connectors and a fully open canvas. With hindsight, I'd ship 6–8 high-value insurance templates first (new-quote evaluation, claim triage, fraud flagging, endorsement review) plus specialised building blocks (policy states, endorsements, regulatory hold steps) before adding general connectors. A guided "agent creation wizard" with progressive disclosure would have unlocked first-success faster than the open canvas did. Today I'd treat templates as the on-ramp, canvas as the power-mode.

04

Observability turned out to be the product.

The run-logs view ended up more used than the canvas itself. Compliance demanded it. Auditors required it. Users loved it. The thing I thought of as a debugging utility was, for the actual buyers and approvers, the headline feature. Next time I'd treat logs/trace/replay as a v1 first-class surface, not a backlog item.

InsureFlow · AI Agent Builder

UX · Product Leadership · Fintech

VoltFi

A US payments app redesign for trust, accessibility, and speed.

Led the UX and product redesign of a mobile banking app for 500K+ retail, student, and SME users, from a fragmented, untrusted experience to a WCAG 2.1 AA-compliant, trust-centric product that beat every KPI set at the start. Stakeholder unnamed for confidentiality.

Role
UX / Product Manager, Lead
Timeline
8 months · 2024
Scale
500K+ users · iOS · Android · Desktop
Team
UX · Eng · QA · Compliance · Support
Tools
Figma
35%
Fewer support tickets
Plain-English fee preview eliminated the #1 ticket driver — "why was I charged this?" — within 6 weeks of launch.
18%
More completed payments
3-step confirmation + live fee preview reduced drop-off at the Send screen from 22% to under 9%.
4.1★
App rating (was 2.9)
App Store rating lifted from 2.9 → 4.1 in 3 months post-launch. Top review theme shifted from "confusing fees" to "finally simple".
92%
Accessibility score
WCAG 2.1 AA across all screens. ≥44px touch targets, 4.5:1 contrast minimum — scored via automated + manual audit.
01 · The Problem

A 22% payment drop-off, a 2.9 rating, and 35% of support tickets about fees nobody could understand

VoltFi had 500K active users and a growing acquisition funnel, but was haemorrhaging trust at the moment it mattered most: the final payment confirmation screen. Heatmaps showed users repeatedly tapping the Help icon before hitting Send. The app felt clinical, unclear on fees, and had never been designed for the Routing–Routing Number corridor that many users relied on for wire transfers.

💸
Hidden Fee Anxiety
Students feared "Transfer" would instantly deduct without showing the exchange rate. 30–40% of support tickets: "Why was I charged this?"
🔁
Multi-Channel Chaos
SME owners had to switch between desktop and mobile to complete verification. Status labels differed per channel: "Processing" on web vs "Pending" on app.
🌍
Terminology Gap
Terminology felt "too corporate". Users looked for "Routing & Account", "ACH Transfer", "Receipt", not "Account Details", "Recurring Payment", "Transaction Receipt".
02 · Research & Discovery

Dual-track research: 12 remote interviews (6 SME owners, 6 students) + analytics deep-dive. I co-facilitated sessions and supervised a WCAG heuristic audit. Findings were synthesised into a shared problem space presented to product, engineering, compliance, and support, before a single wireframe was drawn.

👩‍🎓
Maya, College Student
Sends money to family monthly

"I always screenshot before I hit Send. I need to see exactly what gets taken before I commit."

Pain: No fee preview · Goal: See total before committing
👨‍💼
Marcus, Small Business Owner
12-person e-commerce business

"I run payroll on my laptop but have to verify on my phone. I've had ACH payments show 'pending' for 3 days with zero explanation."

Pain: Cross-device friction · Goal: Single source of truth on status
Analytics + Research Insights
A-0122% drop-off at confirmation screen, heatmaps show Help icon tapped repeatedly before Send
A-0230–40% of support tickets relate to unclear fee disclosure or "failed transfer" with no explanation
R-01Users use familiar mental models: ACH, Routing Number, Zelle, Direct Deposit, not generic fintech terms
R-02WCAG audit: contrast ratio below 3:1 in 12 UI areas, touch targets <44px on 8 key actions
Competitive analysis

Feature parity vs. 5 incumbent US retail banks

Available Not available N/ANot applicable

Mapped 17 retail-banking features across the top 5 US banks to identify gaps VoltFi could exploit. Findings shaped the trust + transparency pillar.

Feature Bank 1 Bank 2 Bank 3 Bank 4 Bank 5 VoltFi
Account Overview
Personalised Dashboard View N/A N/A N/A
Instant Money Transfer (P2P)
Set Recurring Transfers
Set Spending Limit (Credit/Debit)
Add Payee by Photo of Bill
Add Payee by Account or Social Token
Instant Access to Popular Payments
Predefined Default Bills
Payment Reminders
Clear Disclosure of Transfer Fees
Scheduling Automatic Payments
File Transaction Dispute
Paperless Bills & Statements
Monitor Spending Habits
Temporary Hold on Card
Plain-English Fee Preview
Bank 1–5 = top 5 US retail banks by deposit volume, anonymised for portfolio. Data captured Q1 2024 via live app audit + public feature documentation.
Gaps we exploited

Three incumbents lacked instant P2P transfer; four lacked plain-English fee preview. VoltFi built both into the core Send Money flow — making fee transparency our trust pillar's anchor feature.

Primary persona

Jennifer Anderson — the “traveling planner”

🧘‍♀️
Jennifer Anderson
30 · Yoga Teacher · Los Angeles
"Live with no excuses and travel with no regrets, life is too short."
Income$70K EducationMBA FamilySingle ArchetypeTraveller
Technology expertise
Internet · basicadvanced
Mobile apps · basicadvanced
Bio

Yoga teacher who travels 1–3 weekends a month, mostly within the US. Loves a carefree but organised life. Her goal: pay every utility bill on time while she's on the road — and trust that the payment actually goes through before she boards a flight.

Behaviours
  • Frequent traveller
  • Sometimes forgetful
  • Organised by routine
  • Responsible payer
Goals
  • Pay bills on time
  • Avoid late fees
  • Everything on direct debit
  • Fast, reliable payments
  • Confirmation receipts
Frustrations
  • Can't pay easily on the road
  • No way to schedule payments ahead
  • Misses bills, hits late fees
Empathy map

What Jennifer thinks, feels, does, and hears

Synthesised from 1:1 interview sessions during discovery. Drove the “trust + transparency” design pillar.

💭 Thinking
  • "I'm still in a struggling phase and need to save more."
  • "Utility bills stress me out because I forget the due dates."
  • "I want to be more organised about finances."
  • "I want to improve my credit score."
❤️ Feeling
  • Frustrated by late-payment fees; they break the savings plan.
  • Comes home from a trip and is left with low balance.
  • Worried about credit-score impact.
⚡ Pains
  • Late / extra fees raise her stress.
  • Disrupts financial planning.
  • Feels irresponsible.
  • Loses confidence in her discipline.
✨ Gains
  • Pay bills from anywhere.
  • No fees.
  • Saves time.
  • Feels secure.
  • Tracks her spending.
  • Electronic transfers are easier than checks.
🚶‍♀️ Doing
  • Books tickets to different cities / countries.
  • Travels to neighbouring cities most weekends.
  • Stays in hotels.
  • Runs day-long yoga workshops.
  • Travels back once the workshop is over.
  • Tries to set up a routine in between trips.
👂 Hearing
  • "Your late fee is increasing day by day."
  • "This will affect your credit score."
  • "Change banks to live a stress-free life."
How this shaped the design

Three of Jennifer's pains map directly to fee anxiety — which became the wedge for VoltFi's plain-English fee preview, status-clear payment receipts, and "pay-while-travelling" scheduling features.

Customer journey map

Jennifer pays an electricity bill for the first time on mobile

Jennifer's goal
  • Make an electricity bill payment.
  • Has banked since 18, always desktop login.
  • First time trying the banking app.
Expectations

Pay seamlessly, in a well-informed manner. Wants to know what's happening at each step.

Login
Add Payee
Pay Bill
Touchpoints
📱
📱
📱
Goals
A one-stop online banking solution on mobile.
Add electricity provider as a payee.
Pay the electricity provider bill.
Doing
Signs up to the mobile app using existing credentials.
1. Finds the add-payee button.
2. Taps “Add Payee”.
3. Enters company details.
4. Submits the payee.
1. Selects the provider.
2. Enters the date.
3. Enters the amount.
4. Makes the payment.
Thinking
"I need to see what features this app offers — and whether I can pay all my utilities here. I'm glad login was easy."
"Adding a payee is tricky. I hope it's one-time. I hope my entries are right. I didn't get any validation that the company was added."
"When will the payment actually go through? How is the transfer fee calculated? I wish I could schedule this in advance."
Feeling
😊
😐
😞
Pain Points
Login process feels very traditional.
No clear confirmation that the information was added.
Needs transparency in transfer fees, exact payment date, and a way to set up early payment to avoid late fees.
Opportunity
Add Face ID login.
Strengthen authentication.
Auto-populate popular companies.
Validate payee after add.
Show clear fees + precise transfer date.
Enable autopay for scheduled dates.
Set payment reminders.
From journey to design

The emotional drop at “Pay Bill” — fee uncertainty + no clear schedule — drove three product bets: fee preview on the confirmation screen, scheduled payments as a first-class feature, and payment-status clarity throughout.

Information architecture

High-level user flow

A high-level flow defines the specifications and constraints for the next stage of the process. Maps the full task surface across login, payments, cards, and ancillary services.

Start Process Decision Yes No
What this told us

Six task surfaces, three exits per surface (Make / Schedule / Auto). Surfacing the “Schedule” option uniformly across Transfer, Bill Pay, and Add Payee became the architectural commitment — it’s the option Jennifer wanted but couldn’t find in the legacy app.

03 · Strategic Goals & KPIs

I defined three strategic pillars and locked in measurable KPIs before design kicked off, so success wasn't ambiguous at launch.

Pillar 1
Trust & Transparency
Clear fee disclosure, status labels, and 3-step confirmation. Target: 30% fewer support tickets about failed transfers.
Pillar 2
Accessibility & Compliance
WCAG 2.1 AA throughout. ≥44px touch targets, 4.5:1 contrast minimum. Target: 80%+ usability test pass rate.
Pillar 3
Reduce Operational Cost
Fewer support escalations, higher task completion. Target: 15% increase in completed Send Money tasks.
07 · Key Design Decisions
Decision 1

3-Step "Send Money" Wizard, progressive disclosure

Replaced the single cluttered form with three focused screens: Recipient → Amount & fee breakdown → Biometric confirmation. The fee card is non-optional, it cannot be dismissed, only acknowledged. This directly addressed A-01 (confirmation drop-off) and R-01 (trust).

Outcome: 18% increase in completed payments in 3-month beta. Users reported "I can actually tell if a payment has gone through."
Step 1
Recipient
→
Step 2
Fee: $1.90
Total: $381.90
Confirm
→
Step 3
✓
Payment Sent.
Need a receipt?
Decision 2

Trust-Centric Microcopy, clarity over banking-speak

Partnered with content designers to rewrite every label, error, and CTA. The rule: a 16-year-old and a 60-year-old should both understand this instantly.

Before
❌ "Transaction Finalized."
❌ "Account Details"
❌ "Initiate Transfer"
❌ "Send Confirmation Email"
❌ "Recurring Payment"
After
✓ "Payment Sent. Need a receipt?"
✓ "View Routing & Account"
✓ "Send Money"
✓ "Get a receipt"
✓ "ACH Transfer"
Error messages rewritten too: "We couldn't send this payment. Check your balance and try again.", not "Transfer failed. Error code: TX_409."
Decision 3

"VoltFi Navy" Design System, WCAG 2.1 AA throughout

Defined a blue-dominant UI system: deep Maroon (#003366) for primary actions (7:1 contrast against white, well above the 4.5:1 minimum), light blue cards for surface, dark-blue typography. ≥44px touch targets enforced via component library.

WCAG accessibility score: 92% (up from 64%). Dark mode added with WCAG-compliant contrast. Design system adopted across mobile, web, and in-branch.
VoltFi Navy
#0C4A6E · 7:1 contrast
Surface Blue
#F0F0F0 · cards
Navy Type
#001833 · headings
Success Green
#22C55E · sent states
Decision 4

Multi-Channel Harmony, one status vocabulary across all touchpoints

Defined a single source of truth for transaction status and reference numbers, standardised across mobile, web portal, and in-branch kiosk. No more "Processing" on web vs "Pending" on app.

Channel Focus Key Feature
📱 Mobile App Speed & Security FaceID / TouchID Quick-Send, live status tags
💻 Web Portal Reporting & Bulk Tasks CSV export, bulk payment management
🏦 In-Branch Kiosk Identity & Trust QR-code + Plaid identity verification, same ref numbers
08 · Outcomes & Impact
User quotes post-launch
"Payments feel more transparent now. I know exactly what I'm sending and what it'll cost."
"I can actually tell if a payment has gone through. Before I'd just have to wait and hope."
06 · Mobile Experience

Eight-screen iOS flow: login to autopay setup

The mobile experience covers the most repeated tasks: signing in, viewing balance, paying a bill, adding a new payee, configuring autopay, and managing cards — adapted for one-handed use and biometric-first authentication.

Portfolio re-creation · production screens are behind NDA

01 · Sign In
Biometric-first authentication with passcode fallback. Username is pre-filled from device memory — users tap once, Face ID handles the rest.
Mobile design principles

Three commitments shaped every screen: one-thumb reachability (primary CTAs in the bottom 60%), no nested modals (configure-and-confirm on a single scroll), and explicit money state (every screen shows what will happen and when).

06b · Desktop Web App

Full feature set — sidebar nav, bulk pay, analytics

Same VoltFi design language expanded for desktop. Persistent left sidebar, full transaction table with export, bulk bill pay, scheduled transfers, spending analytics, and advanced card controls not available on mobile. Click sidebar nav · ★ = desktop-only features.

Interactive walkthrough below · This is a portfolio re-creation built in HTML/CSS to illustrate the flow. The production product is behind employer NDA.

07 · Leadership Takeaways
Clarity is kindness
In fintech, "slick" UI is secondary to "certain" UI. Users need to know exactly what happens when they tap Send. The fee card alone drove the metric.
Accessibility is a business lever
WCAG compliance wasn't "nice to have", it directly reduced compliance escalations and improved NPS. Made the case to leadership in business terms, not design terms.
12 · Next Steps

Design is an iterative and interactive process

We tested this experience on a cohort of our users and learned some important things. I found many other challenges to focus on for later, such as:

  • How might we help by creating budgeting
  • How might we help with insurance deals
VoltFi · Mobile Banking Redesign
© 2026 Priyanka Nawale Chavan UX Design × AI Engineering Built with intention
  • All Work
UX · Healthcare Analytics · Data Visualisation

PayorIQ

A role-adaptive healthcare performance dashboard for payor reporting.

Healthcare analytics teams were losing 8+ hours per report to manual data pulls and fragmented systems — a direct cost to clinical decision speed. This was a strategic initiative to eliminate that bottleneck: redesigning a spreadsheet-heavy payor reporting system into a role-adaptive performance dashboard that put actionable insight in front of the right person in under 30 minutes.

Role
Product Designer
Timeline
2 years · 2 countries
Domain
Healthcare · B2B SaaS · Payor Analytics
Team
15 people · Data Science · Engineering · UX
Tools
Power BI · Figma · FigJam · Microsoft Fabric
95%
Time reduction
Report generation cut from 8+ hours to under 30 minutes through automated data aggregation.
75%
Faster decisions
Interactive visualisations gave analysts a clear starting point — no more cross-referencing spreadsheets.
99.9%
Data accuracy
Automated aggregation eliminated manual entry errors — analysts finally trusted the numbers they were seeing.
4
Role views shipped
Physician, Admin/CFO, Quality Manager, Analyst — same data layer, personalised lens for each role.
01 · The Problem

Healthcare analysts were drowning in fragmented data, complex spreadsheets, and reports that didn't tell them what to do next

Healthcare providers generate vast amounts of financial and clinical data across disconnected payor systems. Analysts spent hours reconciling spreadsheets just to compare payor performance metrics — the process was time-consuming, error-prone, and produced no visual insights. Leadership needed to understand payor performance to make contracting and resource decisions, but the existing reporting gave them raw numbers with no context, no hierarchy, and no next step.

🕐
Time-Intensive Process
Manual data compilation from multiple systems taking 8+ hours per report.
Impact: Delayed decision-making and reduced productivity
📊
Limited Data Visualisation
Static spreadsheets providing no interactive analysis capabilities.
Impact: Difficulty identifying trends and making comparisons
👥
Inconsistent User Experience
Different interfaces and workflows across various healthcare systems.
Impact: Steep learning curve and increased error rates
⚠
Data Accuracy Concerns
Manual data entry and formula errors in complex calculations.
Impact: Reduced confidence in reporting accuracy
02 · Research & Discovery

Our research went beyond traditional methods, leveraging the expertise of our data team to validate our findings. Data scientists and data engineers analysed vast datasets of user interactions and transactional histories — providing a quantitative backbone to our qualitative work. I conducted contextual inquiry sessions with healthcare analysts in their actual environment, watching them navigate legacy tools in real time.

⊞
Product Foundation
Develop product understanding with research
  • Exploratory Research
  • Pain Point Identification
  • Current Workflow Analysis
  • Feature Prioritisation
⊟
Competitive Analysis
Analysed 6 healthcare analytics platforms and reporting tools.
  • Feature Comparison
  • UX Benchmarking
  • Market Gap Analysis
📈
Analytics Review
Analysed current system usage patterns and user behaviour
  • User Journey Mapping
  • Drop-off Point Analysis
  • Feature Utilisation
🖥
Stakeholder Workshops
Facilitated workshops with stakeholders to gather requirements.
  • Requirement Gathering
  • Process Mapping
  • Success Metrics Definition
👥
Cross-Functional Collaboration
Integration of Data Science and Data Engineering in UX Design
  • Actionable Insights
  • Predictive Analytics
  • Smart KPIs

The research yielded a detailed understanding of how healthcare analysts work, revealing their specific needs, challenges, and key workflow requirements — crucial for designing a more effective performance reporting system.

⊞
Data Fragmentation

Analysts spend 65% of their time aggregating data from 5+ different systems.

↗
Visualisation Needs

Manual report generation is time-consuming, limiting the number of evaluations that can be completed monthly.

🗄
Comparison Challenges

Siloed workflows prevent effective collaboration between analysts, administrators and clinical teams.

03 · Project Goal

Clear objectives focused on improving analyst productivity, data accuracy, and decision-making capabilities in healthcare performance reporting.

⚡
Improve Efficiency

Reduce report generation time from 8+ hours to under 30 minutes

Target: 95% time reduction
↗
Enhanced Decision Making

Provide interactive visualisations for better payor performance insights

Target: 75% faster decisions
🛡
Data Accuracy

Eliminate manual errors through automated data aggregation

Target: 99.9% accuracy
🎯
User Adoption

Achieve high user satisfaction and platform adoption rates

Target: 90% adoption target
04 · Empathy Mapping & Persona

Understanding the healthcare analyst's perspective through their thoughts, feelings, observations, and actions during the reporting process.

💡
Thinks & Believes
  • I need to get this report done quickly
  • The data from different systems doesn't match
  • There must be a better way to do this
  • I hope I didn't make any calculation errors
🤍
Feels
  • Frustrated with manual processes
  • Stressed about deadlines
  • Uncertain about data accuracy
  • Overwhelmed by complexity
👁
Sees
  • Multiple spreadsheets and systems
  • Colleagues struggling with similar tasks
  • Executives waiting for insights
  • Outdated reporting interfaces
💬
Says & Does
  • Manually extracts data from 5+ systems
  • Creates complex Excel formulas
  • Double-checks calculations multiple times
  • Reformats data for presentations

By creating personas, the team gained a shared, empathetic understanding of their users' goals and challenges.

👨‍💼
Michael Rodriguez
Healthcare Administrator
Age: 56
"We need data-driven insights to make strategic decisions."
Goals
  • Monitor departmental KPIs
  • Ensure regulatory compliance
  • Optimise resource allocation
Frustrations
  • Siloed data sources
  • Manual reporting processes
  • Delayed insights
Needs
  • Comprehensive analytics platform
  • Automated compliance reporting
  • Cost-effectiveness metrics
05 · Information Architecture

Moved from a flat, overwhelming report list to a 4-level structured hierarchy. The platform's IA was designed around a logical progression: Overview Dashboard (high-level KPI summary for quick scanning), Detailed Metrics (individual metric pages with drill-down for payor comparisons), Personalised Reports (custom saved reports per role), and a Resource Centre (best-practice guidance and benchmarks). The same underlying data, surfaced through the right lens for each user.

Command Centre first
L1 Overview gives every role the same 30-second pulse. Depth is opt-in, not forced. Reduced initial cognitive load by 60% in usability tests.
Role-adaptive, not role-locked
Users switch roles within a session. A quality manager might need the CFO view for a board meeting. Role selector stays visible at all times.
Insights as L1 navigation
Most dashboards bury anomaly alerts in settings. Making Insights top-level nav was the single most praised decision in post-launch interviews.
Design Goal

The design process was iterative and focused on a user-centric approach, starting with low-fidelity wireframes and moving toward a high-fidelity prototype.

🎨 Clarity & Simplicity
Clear headings, intuitive icons, and a minimalist colour palette to reduce cognitive load.
📊 Visual-First
Rely on data visualisations to tell a story at a glance. Charts surface insight before tables.
👤 Personalisation
Role-based logins surface personalised metrics — a doctor sees different defaults to a CFO.
06 · Data Visualisation & Lo-Fi Wireframes

Based on insights from empathy mapping, we focused on simplifying the user journey for healthcare analysts by choosing specific chart types to represent different data points effectively:

📊 KPI Cards
Large cards at the top display key indicators like "Average Visit Time" or "Patient Satisfaction Score" with a trend arrow indicating change over time.
📈 Line Graphs
Used to show performance trends over weeks, months, or years — crucial for providers like Dr. Reed to see progress and compare parameters at a glance.
📊 Bar Charts
Ideal for comparing performance between different providers or clinics at a single point in time.
💡 Actionable Insights
A simple algorithm analyses provider data and surfaces personalised suggestions inline — e.g. "Provider MedCorp has underperformed for Quarter 1, click to see details."
07 · Key Design Decisions
Decision 1

Semantic colour system, built from scratch, trusted by users

The existing system used colour inconsistently, red didn't always mean bad, green didn't always mean good. Users had learned to ignore it. We rebuilt a strict semantic palette: Green = on track, Amber = watch, Red = act now, Blue = informational. Applied consistently across every chart, card, and alert.

On track
Watch
Act now
Informational
→ Time to interpret a screen: 4 min → 45 sec
Decision 2

Actionable Insights Engine, from observation to next step

The differentiator. Instead of showing a red bar, the system tells you why it's red and what to do. The engine detects anomalies, correlates them against staffing or volume changes, then surfaces a "Next Step" CTA.

Example insight, live in production
DETECT
Cardiology readmission rate up 5% over 6 weeks
CORRELATE
Aligned with 18% staffing reduction in same dept, Aug–Sep
NEXT STEP
"Review Cardiology Staffing Levels" → "View Post-Op Best Practices"
Decision 3

Visual-first KPI hierarchy, sparklines over spreadsheets

Every metric gets a sparkline and a trend percentage on the overview. If a clinician looks at nothing else, they see the 4 most vital numbers for their role in under 5 seconds. Drill-down is available but never forced. This single change drove the 50% reduction in time-to-insight.

08 · Outcomes
50% faster to key metrics
Measured via task timing in moderated usability tests, find your top KPI in under 30 seconds
35% increase in clinician MAU
Clinicians finally had a view built for their workflow, not the CFO's workflow
10% appointment utilisation improvement
Early pilot data, directly traced to the Operational Efficiency view surfacing scheduling gaps
4 role views shipped at launch
Physician, Admin/CFO, Quality Manager, Analyst, all personalised, all on the same data layer
User quotes post-launch
"For the first time I can see in 30 seconds whether my department is on track without opening a single spreadsheet."
"The insight engine flagged a readmission trend I'd missed for two months. We adjusted staffing and it reversed in 3 weeks."
09 · Reflection
Design for action, not observation
The most beautiful chart is useless if the user doesn't know what to do next. Every screen asks: "what decision does this enable?"
Observe in context, not a lab
Watching a cardiologist navigate during a shift handover revealed pain that no interview question would have surfaced. Contextual inquiry was non-negotiable here.
Consistency as trust
Users had learned to distrust the old colour system. Rebuilding that trust meant being 100% consistent, even when a single exception felt justified.
What I'd do differently
Involve compliance and clinical governance earlier. The Insights Engine required two extra sprint cycles for approval, had I looped them in at research stage, we'd have shipped 3 weeks sooner.
10 · A/B Testing & Iteration

Data-driven design decisions based on user behaviour analysis and controlled experiments with healthcare analysts.

Test
Winner
Metric
Confidence
Lift
Navigation Layout
Sidebar vs. top nav
Top Nav
Task Completion Rate
95%
↗ +23%
Chart Type
Bar vs. line charts
Line Charts
Data Comprehension
92%
↗ +18%
Filter Placement
Left panel vs. inline
Inline
Filter Usage Rate
98%
↗ +31%
Color Coding
Status vs. payor-based
Status-based
Error Recognition
89%
↗ +15%
156
Participants
12
Tests
27%
Avg Lift
↗ Top nav improved discoverability by 23%
↘ Sidebar nav confused mobile users
↗ Inline filters increased usage by 31%
↘ Bar charts less effective for trends
↗ Line charts enhanced trend comprehension
↘ Left panel filters were often overlooked
↗ Status colours reduced errors by 15%
↘ Payor colours caused misinterpretation

Basis A/B test results, wireframes were iterated and the high-fidelity designs below were produced.

11 · High-Fidelity Designs

The final designs translated research and A/B learnings into a production-ready provider performance reporting platform — with real provider data, inline insights, multi-metric trend charts, and a drill-down detail panel.

08 · Next Steps
🔬
Data Science & Predictive Modeling
Build models to forecast patient outcomes based on provider metrics, enabling proactive interventions before performance dips become crises. Correlation and anomaly detection to auto-flag unusual patterns.
📱
Mobile Access & Agile MVP
Build an MVP with core features to gather user feedback fast, then iterate. Establish secure data connections and a roadmap for mobile access — clinicians need a 30-second pulse check they can run between patients on any device.
👥
Expanded Personas & Specialist Coverage
Interview additional medical specialists across different fields to ensure the platform adapts to roles beyond the initial 4. Longitudinal usability studies to validate that the design positively shifts behaviour over time.
PayorIQ, Healthcare Performance Dashboard
© 2026 Priyanka Nawale Chavan UX Design × AI Engineering Built with intention
  • All Work
AI Engineering · Design Tools

UX Alchemy

An AI-powered UX audit platform for early-stage design teams.

A Streamlit app that ingests PDFs, images, HTML/CSS, and ZIP archives, then runs a structured multi-agent audit against Nielsen, WCAG, Gestalt, and cognitive-load heuristics. Built solo in 6 weeks. CrewAI orchestrates the agent crew; LangGraph manages the audit state graph.

Role
Solo · Design + AI Engineering
Timeline
~6 weeks part-time · 2026
Stack
Python · Streamlit · CrewAI · LangGraph · GPT-4o
Status
v1 shipped · Streamlit Cloud
Tools
VS Code · GitHub · Cursor
6
Weeks to ship
Solo build, part-time. From idea to v1 live on Streamlit Cloud.
4
Heuristic frameworks
Nielsen · WCAG 2.1 · Gestalt · Cognitive load — all evaluated per upload.
6+
File formats
PDF, PNG, HTML/CSS, Markdown, JSON, ZIP — OCR via Tesseract for screenshots.
🏆
Best Innovation
EY GDS FY26 TD AI Event · Best Innovative Solution Award.
01 · The Problem

UX audits don't scale, but the work is mostly pattern-matching.

A real UX audit takes a senior designer 2-5 days per product surface. They open figma files, click through prototypes, run accessibility tools, cross-reference Nielsen heuristics, write up findings, and prioritize fixes. Output is a 30-page report — most of it is the same 10 categories of finding rephrased for that product.

The repetitive part — pattern-matching design artefacts against established heuristics — is exactly what LLMs are good at. The judgement part — knowing which 3 fixes to prioritise this sprint — still needs a human. UX Alchemy is the bet that automating the first part frees the human to spend more time on the second.

02 · System Architecture

A LangGraph state machine, with CrewAI agents inside each node.

The audit runs as a LangGraph state graph. Each node is a callable that reads from UXProjectState, runs a CrewAI Crew internally, and writes back the result. Two reusable agents — a Strategist and an Auditor — get assigned different tasks at different nodes.

Stack & state, in code

CrewAI for agents, LangChain text splitters for chunking long inputs, LangGraph for the state graph. UXProjectState is a TypedDict that every node reads from and writes back to.

import fitz   # PyMuPDF
import docx
from PIL import Image
import pytesseract

# LangChain / CrewAI / LangGraph
from crewai import Agent, Task, Crew, Process, LLM
from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
from langchain_core.messages import HumanMessage
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END

USE_OPENAI = True   # Set False to use Gemini

Key tradeoffs

Decision Picked Considered Why
Agent framework CrewAI LangChain agents · AutoGen Role-based agent specs map naturally to UX audit roles. Less boilerplate than raw LangChain.
Orchestration LangGraph Linear chain · CrewAI sequential Audit logic branches (e.g. skip a11y pass if no images uploaded). State graph models branches cleanly.
OCR Tesseract GPT-4o Vision · Google Vision API Local, free, reliable on text-heavy screenshots. Vision APIs would 3× the cost per audit.
LLM GPT-4o (default) · Gemini optional Claude · GPT-4o-mini Single USE_OPENAI flag flips the backend. Lets the same code path serve both models for comparison.
Frontend Streamlit Next.js · Gradio · FastAPI + React Solo-dev speed beats production polish for v1. File-upload UX is free, deploy in one command.
03 · Agent Design

Two reusable agents, multiple tasks.

Rather than creating a new agent for every audit step, the system defines two reusable agents — a Strategist (user-research + synthesis) and an Auditor (heuristic critique + scoring). Each LangGraph node binds these agents to a different Task with its own description and expected output. Fewer agents = simpler prompts = easier to debug.

S Strategist
Role · UX research + synthesis

Used in research_librarian_node to generate baseline user-research insights, and again in UXAuditEngine to analyse research against the uploaded design.

A Auditor
Role · Heuristic critique + scoring

Audits the design against research findings and Nielsen / WCAG criteria. Returns structured JSON with scores, GAPS, Roadmap, and a 9-quadrant spatial_feedback map.

A LangGraph node: research_librarian_node

Every node has the same shape: read from state, build a Task, spin up a Crew, return a partial state dict. Includes a defensive "summarize if too long" step to keep downstream prompts in budget.

def research_librarian_node(state: UXProjectState) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    task = Task(
        description="Generate initial user research insights for a general UX project.",
        expected_output="A summary of typical user needs for intuitive navigation and usability.",
        agent=strategist,
    )
    crew = Crew(agents=[strategist], tasks=[task], process=Process.sequential)
    result = crew.kickoff()
    research_text = str(result)

    # Summarize if too long
    if len(research_text) > 500:
        summary_task = Task(
            description=f"Summarize the following research: {research_text}",
            expected_output="Concise summary of key user insights.",
            agent=strategist,
        )
        crew = Crew(agents=[strategist], tasks=[summary_task], process=Process.sequential)
        research_text = str(crew.kickoff())

    return {"research_summary": research_text}
04 · Structured Output

Heuristics encoded as task instructions; JSON parsed leniently.

A free-form "review this UX" prompt produces vague output. Structure changes that. The Auditor's task description spells out, step by step, what to evaluate against — Nielsen's heuristics, WCAG, 9 spatial quadrants — and what shape the result should be:

Nielsen's 10 Heuristics WCAG (if applicable) 9 Spatial Quadrants Scores · Gaps · Roadmap

The audit Crew: UXAuditEngine.run_audit()

Two tasks, two agents, run sequentially in a single Crew. The Auditor's expected_output is the rubric — it doubles as a schema spec the LLM tries to fit.

# Strategist task: Analyze research
strategy_task = Task(
    description=(
        "1) Analyze the user research data:\n"
        f"{artifact_data.get('research', '')}\n"
        "2) Identify key user needs, pain points, and requirements.\n"
        "3) Summarize gaps that might exist in the design."
    ),
    expected_output="Summary of user research insights and potential gaps.",
    agent=strategist,
)

# Auditor task: Audit design against research and standards
audit_task = Task(
    description=(
        "1) Review the design data:\n"
        f"{artifact_data.get('design', '')}\n"
        "2) Compare with research insights and use misc context if relevant.\n"
        "3) Flag missing features (Gaps) and usability errors.\n"
        "4) Evaluate against Nielsen Heuristics or WCAG if applicable.\n"
        "5) Divide visual feedback into 9 spatial quadrants."
    ),
    expected_output=(
        "Return a JSON object with:\n"
        "- 'scores' (Usability, Alignment, Accessibility, Consistency; 0-10 scale)\n"
        "- 'GAPS' (List of findings)\n"
        "- 'Roadmap' (list of prioritised fixes)"
    ),
    agent=auditor,
)

# Crew with multiple agents
crew = Crew(agents=[strategist, auditor], tasks=[strategy_task, audit_task], process=Process.sequential)
result = crew.kickoff()
return self._parse_json(str(result))

Output parsing is deliberately lenient: re.search(r"\{.*\}", raw_text, re.DOTALL) grabs the first JSON-like block, then json.loads() parses it. If the LLM wraps JSON in markdown fences or chats around it, this still works. If the JSON itself is malformed, the call returns None and the node short-circuits with a logged warning rather than crashing.

05 · What it Generates

Five sections, scannable in 60 seconds.

The Streamlit UI displays results across tabs so users can jump to the section they need without reading the full report. JSON export is one click away.

Overall Score
72 /100
Weighted across 5 dimensions
Gap Analysis
Structured findings table — heuristic_id, severity, evidence, suggested fix.
Accessibility Review
WCAG 2.1 AA pass rates per criterion. Flags contrast failures, missing alt-text, focus order.
Recommendations
Top-5 prioritised fixes — severity × effort scored. Concrete next actions, not just observations.
06 · Eval + Limitations

What works, what doesn't.

I tested the system on a handful of real product surfaces — portfolio sites, SaaS landing pages, a fintech checkout flow — and compared the audit output against my own senior-UX review of the same surface. The comparison is informal but instructive.

What it does well
  • Catches the majority of the issues a senior reviewer would flag on a first pass, especially structural and content-hierarchy problems.
  • Surfaces accessibility gaps (contrast, missing labels, focus order) more reliably than a human on long pages — fatigue isn't a factor.
  • Output is broadly consistent across runs on the same input — variance is low enough to be useful for tracking changes between design iterations.
  • JSON export drops cleanly into design tickets, so findings move from "report PDF" to "actionable task" in one click.
Where it struggles
  • Layout-aware critique. OCR loses spatial relationships. The model sees text but not "is the CTA above the fold." Spatial-quadrant prompts help, but vision input is the real fix. On roadmap.
  • Long PDFs. Multi-page design docs hit context limits. Text is chunked with RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter, but cross-chunk reasoning is weak.
  • Domain-specific UX. Generic heuristics don't catch finance-specific patterns (e.g. trust signals in checkout flows). Needs vertical rubrics layered on top of Nielsen.
  • Hallucinated findings. The Auditor will occasionally cite evidence that isn't in the input. The lenient JSON parser passes them through; there's no cross-checker agent yet, so caveat-emptor on the longer GAPS lists.
07 · What I'd Build in V2

Three bets, ranked.

1
Vision-first critique pass

Replace OCR-only image handling with GPT-4o Vision for layout, hierarchy, and visual-design critique. The biggest current gap.

2
Vertical rubrics

Fintech, healthcare, e-commerce each have UX patterns that generic heuristics miss. Add domain-specific rubrics, let users pick at upload time.

3
Figma + Linear integrations

Pipe audit findings directly into design tickets. Closes the loop from "audit ran" to "fix shipped."
